coredata.dc:title: Evaluation of hemp stem retting water for carbon dots production and their phyto-activity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Jiafeng
Ouyang, Wenjing
Chen, Gonglin
Yuan, Song
Ji, Chunyu
Du, Guanghui
Hussain, Saddam
Peng, Zhili
Tang, Kailei
coredata.dc:description: Water-retting hemp stem yields high-quality fiber but generates wastewater causing environmental pollution. In the present study, the retting water of hemp stem was used to synthesize carbon dots (HP-CDs). The obtained HP-CDs not only exhibited similar fluorescence emission characteristics to that of widely studied citric acid-based CDs (CA-CDs) but also contained nitrogen elements, primarily presented in the form of graphitic and pyridinic structures. Furthermore, the effect of HP-CDs on hemp growth and physiological characteristics were examined at six concentration levels (0, 10, 100, 500, 1000, and 2000 mg⋅L−1) in comparison with those of CA-CDs. Results indicated that HP-CDs were non-phytotoxic across the tested concentration levels. In contrast, CA-CDs exhibited concentration-dependent phytotoxicity, with concentration higher than 500 mg L−1 resulted in adverse effects, including increased activity of superoxide dismutase (SOD) and increased concentration of malondialdehyde (MDA) in leaves, necrotic symptom at the leaf edges, reduced root volume, and decreased dry weight accumulation. In a subsequent experiment, plants grown aeroponically with 100 mg L−1 of HP-CDs promoted nitrogen uptake, and resulted in a higher leaf and stem dry weight than the plants at 0 mg L−1 by 36.4 % and 37.8 %, respectively, which was comparable with the effects of CA-CDs. In conclusion, CDs derived from hemp retting water exhibited low biological toxicity and demonstrated similar plant growth-promoting effect as chemically derived CDs. Therefore, hemp stem retting water holds potential as a source for carbon dot production, and thereby supporting the sustainable hemp industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Industrial Hemp (Cannabis sativa L.)
Stem retting water
Biomass based carbon dots
Biological activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots from citric acid and hemp stem retting water
Characteristics of carbon dots synthesized from citric acid and hemp stem retting water
Effects of carbon dots synthesized from citric acid and hemp stem retting water on the growth of hemp seedlings
Effects of carbon dots on the physiological and biochemical characteristics of hemp seedling
Effects of carbon dots on nitrogen uptake in hemp seedlings
Characteristics of carbon dots derived from hemp stem retting water
Carbon dots derived from hemp stem retting water were less phytotoxic than citric acid-derived
Carbon dots derived from hemp stem retting water promoted plant growth

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Plants cultivated in an aeroponics cultivation system.
Fluorescence (FL) emission spectrum of HP-CDs (A) and CA-CDs (B). CA-CDs and HP-CDs indicate citric acid-based and hemp-retting-water-based carbon dots, respectively.
Raman spectrum of HP-CDs (A) and CA-CDs (B). CA-CDs and HP-CDs indicate citric acid-based and hemp-retting-water-based carbon dots, respectively.
Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of HP-CDs (A) and CA-CDs (B) CA-CDs and HP-CDs indicate citric acid-based and hemp retting-water-based carbon dots, respectively.

X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS) spectrum of HP-CDs (panels A, B, C, D) and CA-CDs (panels E, F, G). Panels B, C, and D indicate a high-resolution spectrum of C1s, O 1 s, and N 1 s for HP-CDs, respectively. Panels F and G indicate a high-resolution spectrum of C1s, and O 1 s for CA-CDs, respectively. CA-CDs and HP-CDs indicate citric acid-based and hemp-retting-water-based carbon dots, respectively.

Effects of CDs type and concentration on the dry weight of leaf and root of hemp seedlings in Trial 1#. (A) and (B) represent the dry weight of the leaf and panels (C) and (D) represent the weight of the root of seedlings treated with CDs, respectively; Different letters above bars in each panel indicate statistical differences between treatments at p = 0.05. Error bars indicate the standard error (SE) of the mean. CA-CDs and HP-CDs indicate citric acid-based and hemp-retting-water-based carbon dots, respectively. C0, C10, C100, C500, C1000, and C2000 represent CDs concentrations of 0, 10, 100, 500, 1000, and 2000 mg L−1, respectively.

Effects of carbon dots treatment on the leaf morphology of hemp seedlings in Trial 1#. Panels A and B present the roots of ‘J1’ and ‘Y8’ treated with HP-CDs, respectively; Panels C and D present the roots of ‘J1’ and ‘Y8’ treated with CA-CDs, respectively. CA-CDs and HP-CDs indicate citric acid-based and hemp-retting-water-based carbon dots, respectively. C0, C10, C100, C500, C1000, and C2000 represent CDs concentrations of 0, 10, 100, 500, 1000, and 2000 mg L−1, respectively.

Effects of carbon dots treatment on the root morphology of hemp seedlings in trial 1#. Panels (A) and (B) present the roots of ‘J1’ and ‘Y8’ treated with HP-CDs, respectively; Panels (C) and (D) present the roots of ‘J1’ and ‘Y8’ treated with CA-CDs, respectively. CA-CDs and HP-CDs indicate citric acid-based and hemp-retting-water-based carbon dots, respectively. C0, C10, C100, C500, C1000, and C2000 represent CDs concentrations of 0, 10, 100, 500, 1000, and 2000 mg L−1, respectively.

Effects of CD type and concentration on the dry weight of hemp seedlings in Trial 2#. (A) and (B) represent the organ dry weights of c.v. J1 and Y8, respectively. Different letters above bars in each panel indicate statistical differences between treatments at p = 0.05 of each organ. Error bars indicate the standard error (SE) of the mean. CA-CDs and HP-CDs indicate citric acid-based and hemp-retting-water-based carbon dots, respectively. The concentration of C0 was used as a control (CK). The concentration of both CDs type was 100 mg L−1.

Effects of concentration and CDs type on SOD activity and MDA content of hemp seedlings in Trial 1#. Panels (A) and (B) represent SOD activity and Panels C and D represent MDA content respectively. Different letters above bars in each panel indicate statistical differences between treatments at P = 0.05. Error bars indicate the standard error (SE) of the mean. CA-CDs and HP-CDs indicate citric acid-based and hemp-retting-water-based carbon dots, respectively. C0, C10, C100, C500, C1000, and C2000 represent CDs concentrations of 0, 10, 100, 500, 1000, and 2000 mg L−1, respectively.
Composition of the full-concentration nutrient solution applied.
Effect of CDs concentration, CDs type, and cultivar on hemp biomass in Trial 1#.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Data is presented as mean ± standard error (SE). Different letters following numbers in the same column of each category indicate statistical differences at p = 0.05.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of CDs type, concentration, and cultivar on hemp physiological and biochemical traits in Trials 1#.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: SS concentration, SP concentration, SOD Activity, and MDA concentration represent leaf-soluble sugar content, soluble protein content, dismutase activity, and malondialdehyde content, respectively. Data is presented as mean ± standard error (SE). Different letters following numbers in the same column of each category indicate statistical differences at P = 0.05.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen content and uptake in different organs of hemp plants in Trial 2#.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: CA-CDs and HP-CDs indicate citric acid-based and hemp-retting-water-based carbon dots, respectively. Data is presented as mean ± standard error (SE). Different letters following numbers in the same column of each category indicate statistical differences at p = 0.05.

head.ce:title: Evaluation of hemp stem retting water for carbon dots production and their phyto-activity

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this article

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-retting hemp stem yields high-quality fiber but generates wastewater causing environmental pollution. In the present study, the retting water of hemp stem was used to synthesize carbon dots (HP-CDs). The obtained HP-CDs not only exhibited similar fluorescence emission characteristics to that of widely studied citric acid-based CDs (CA-CDs) but also contained nitrogen elements, primarily presented in the form of graphitic and pyridinic structures. Furthermore, the effect of HP-CDs on hemp growth and physiological characteristics were examined at six concentration levels (0, 10, 100, 500, 1000, and 2000 mg⋅L−1) in comparison with those of CA-CDs. Results indicated that HP-CDs were non-phytotoxic across the tested concentration levels. In contrast, CA-CDs exhibited concentration-dependent phytotoxicity, with concentration higher than 500 mg L−1 resulted in adverse effects, including increased activity of superoxide dismutase (SOD) and increased concentration of malondialdehyde (MDA) in leaves, necrotic symptom at the leaf edges, reduced root volume, and decreased dry weight accumulation. In a subsequent experiment, plants grown aeroponically with 100 mg L−1 of HP-CDs promoted nitrogen uptake, and resulted in a higher leaf and stem dry weight than the plants at 0 mg L−1 by 36.4 % and 37.8 %, respectively, which was comparable with the effects of CA-CDs. In conclusion, CDs derived from hemp retting water exhibited low biological toxicity and demonstrated similar plant growth-promoting effect as chemically derived CDs. Therefore, hemp stem retting water holds potential as a source for carbon dot production, and thereby supporting the sustainable hemp industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hemp (Cannabis sativa L.) stem contains high-quality bark fiber, which is used in the production of ropes, specialty paper and pulp, eco-friendly textiles, and biodegradable composites (Crini et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2016). To extract the bark fiber, hemp stems must undergo a retting process to remove non-fibrous components such as pectin, lignin, and wax. The most commonly used methods, dew retting and water retting, both rely on pectic enzymes secreted by indigenous bacteria and fungi (Ribeiro et al., 2015). Water retting is faster, offers better control, and yields more consistent fiber quality than dew retting, while being less dependent on climate conditions (Magnusson and Svennerstedt, 2007; Jankauskienė et al., 2015). However, the anaerobic fermentation in water retting generates polluters (Manian et al., 2021), resulting in large amounts of wastewater. Inappropriate disposal of the retting water could lead to environmental issues. It is therefore needed to explore solutions for value-added utilization of the retting water.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention due to their unique properties, including simple synthesis, tunable photoluminescence, and photostability (Li et al., 2012; Luo et al., 2013; Ozyurt et al., 2023). These properties make CDs advantageous in various application fields, such as bioimaging (Li et al., 2019), sensors (Xu et al., 2020a), and optoelectronic devices (Wang et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2020). Moreover, growth enhancement effects of CDs have been shown across a range of species, including tomato (Chen et al., 2022), mung bean (Xu et al., 2020b), soybean (Wang et al., 2022a), wheat (Tripathi et al., 2015), rice (Li et al., 2020a) and maize (Tan et al., 2021). Nevertheless, the phytotoxicity of CDs can not be neglected. While studies indicate that CDs exhibit low toxicity to plants at low concentrations, the potential for adverse effects increases with higher dosages (Li et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2022). Understanding the concentration-dependent effects of CDs is essential for their safe and effective application in agricultural practices, ensuring that their benefits are maximized while mitigating potential risks to plant health.

While early methods of synthesizing CDs often involve chemical reagents, such as citric acid, ammonium citrate, branched polyethyleneimine, and ethylenediamine (Fernando et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2017), recent researches have highlighted the advantages of using biomass-based resources for CDs synthesis. For instance, Tiwari et al. (2019a); (2019b) successfully synthesized CDs from cannabis leaves and inflorescences, which can function as effective nanosensors and exfoliating agents. Additionally, the nutrients present in biomass-derived CDs have been shown to be beneficial for regulating plant physiological processes (Li et al., 2020b, 2021), further increasing their value in sustainable agricultural practices. Building on this, the use of other agricultural byproducts and biomass wastes from the agricultural sector has expanded the possibilities of sustainable CD synthesis, promoting both sustainability and waste reduction. Wang et al. (2021b) reported that corn stalks, a common agricultural byproduct, can serve as a carbon source for the synthesis of CDs. Similarly, Sun et al. (2020) explored the potential of rice husks, transforming them into phosphorescent CDs that exhibit ultralong lifetime and high efficiency, highlighting the feasibility of using agricultural residues for the production of high-quality CDs.

Hemp stem retting water is rich in organic carbon, making it an excellent candidate precursor for CDs synthesis. The primary objective of this study is to explore the potential of using hemp retting water as a source for CDs production and to investigate how the derived CDs affect plant growth. To better understand the properties of CDs synthesized from the retting-water of hemp stem (HP-CDs), we included CDs synthesized from citric acid (CA-CDs) as a control, as their properties have been well-researched in several studies (Qu et al., 2012; Simões et al., 2016; Lin et al., 2022). Specifically, we aimed to (1) synthesize CDs from the retting water of hemp stems, (2) characterize the physicochemical properties and biological activity of HP-CDs, (3) compare the effect of HP-CDs and CA-CDs on hemp plant growth. The results of this study could provide a solution for value-added utilization of the retting water, presenting a promising avenue for sustainable CDs synthesis and potentially contributing to both environmental and economic sustainability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots from citric acid and hemp stem retting water
ce:section.ce:para: Anhydrous citrate (99 %) was purchased from Energy Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hemp stem retting water was prepared by soaking hemp stems (c.v. Yunma No.8) in tap water for 7 days at 15–20 °C. The retting water was concentrated 10 times by atmospheric distillation in an adjustable temperature multipurpose electric furnace (FD-2000W, Changsha Miqi Instrument Equipment Co., Ltd. China).

Citric acid-based CDs (CA-CDs) were synthesized through hydrothermal method (Wang et al., 2021a). Briefly, 10 g of citric acid was dissolved in 1 mL of deionized water, well mixed, and transferred to a Teflon autocladium to heat at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, orange-yellow colloidal carbon dots were obtained.

HP-CDs were synthesized in a similar manner to CA-CDs. Briefly, 250 mL of concentrated hemp stem retting water was heated at 200 °C for 12 h in a hydrothermal autoclave. The resulting reactants were filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove biomass-based macroaggregates. The obtained solution was dialyzed for 24 h using a molecular weight cutoff value (MWCO) of 3000 in a dialysis bag (Hunan Yibo Biotechnology Co., Ltd. China) to remove the unreacted precursors. The dialyzed solution was then freeze-dried to obtain HP-CDs.

The CA-CDs and HP-CDs were characterized for their fluorescence excitation and emission parameters using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F97 Pro, Shanghai, China). Microscopic confocal Raman spectrometer (inVia, Renishawin, UK) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Middle age Walker, Thermo, USA) were used for structural information, while X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (K-Alpha+, Thermo, USA) was used for elemental information. The morphology of CDs was characterized using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained using an accelerating voltage of 200 kV and a magnification of 800 K times.

Two experiments were conducted in the greenhouse at Yunnan University, Yunnan Province, China (24.82°N, 102.85°E), in November 2021 and August 2022, respectively. In the first experiment (Trial 1#), hemp plants were grown in pots to test the concentration-dependent phyto-bioactivity of CA-CDs and HP-CDs. In the second experiment (Trial 2#), hemp seedlings were cultivated using an aeroponic system to evaluate the effect of CDs on biomass acucmulation and nitrogen uptake in hemp plants. Hemp cultivars ‘Yunma No.8’ (hereafter refers as Y8) and ‘Jinma 1’(hereafter refers as J1) were used in both experiments.

In Trial 1#, hemp seeds were sown in pots (sized 8 cm in height, 4 cm in diameter) filled with φ2 mm quartz sand. After emergence, two uniform seedlings per pot were maintained. Each pot received 100 mL of modified Hoagland nutrient solution ( Table 1) every three days throughout the experiment. At the three-true leaf stage (37 days after sowing), CA-CDs and HP-CDs solutions were applied separately to each pot at six concentration levels: 0 mg L−1 (C0), 10 mg L−1 (C10), 100 mg L−1 (C100), 500 mg L−1 (C500), 1000 mg L−1 (C1000), and 2000 mg L−1 (C2000). Each pot received 100 mL of the respective CDs-contained solution for three times, with a four-day interval between two applications. Each treatment had three replicates.

In Trial 2#, hemp seeds were sown in pot filled with 2 mm quartz sand. At the three-true leaf stage (22 days after sowing), seedlings were transplanted into an aeroponic system ( Fig. 1). In this system, plants were grown in the bucket, and recyclable nutrient solution was sprayed to root for 30 seconds once every 10 minutes. The nutrient solution was replaced every five days. Each bucket contains five cultivation holes with a diameter of 7 cm. Seedlings of two test cultivars were transplanted in one bucket, with each cultivar placed in two cultivation holes, and one seedling per hole. After the plants were transplanted, deionized water was used for acclimatization for 3 days, followed by a half-concentration modified Hoagland nutrient solution for an additional 4 days, and then a full-concentration modified Hoagland nutrient solution (Table 1). Seventeen days after transplanting, two levels of treatments were applied for both CA-CDs and HP-CDs: 0 mg L−1 (C0) and 100 mg L−1 (C100), with the C0 treatment serving as the control. Carbon dots were dissolved in the nutrient solution. Each aeroponic bucket serves as one treatment and one replicate, with all measurement conduced with four replicates for each treatment.

For Trial 1#, plant height (measured from the surface of quartz sand to the stem tip) and stem diameter (measured at 7 cm above the surface of quartz sand) were recorded on the day of the first CDs application and on the third day after the last application. Leaf chlorophyll content was monitored by the SPAD value, using a chlorophyll meter (SPAD-502Plus, Konica minolta, Japan) on the uppermost pair of fully expanded leaves on the third day after the last CDs application. Subsequently, fresh leaves were collected to determine superoxide dismutase activity (SOD), malondialdehyde content (MDA), soluble sugar content (SS), and soluble protein content (SP) by using Nitrogen Blue Tetrazolium method (Hu et al., 2020), Thiobarbituric Acid method (De Vos et al., 1991), Anthrone-sulfuric Acid method (Chen et al., 2014), Coomassie Brilliant Blue method (Ren et al., 2011), respectively. Afterward, the seedlings were uprooted and divided into roots, stems, and leaves, and their dry weights were determined after being dried in an oven at 80 °C for 48 hours.

For Trial 2#, seedlings were harvested five days after the last CDs application. The dry weight of roots, stems and leaves was determined after being dried in an oven at 80 °C for 48 hours. Total nitrogen content (N) in roots, stems and leaves was determined by Kjeldahl’s method (JK9830, China).

To test the significance for the effects of type (CDs), CDs concentration (Conc.), hemp cultivars (CV) and their interactions on each parameter measured in Trial 1#, and the significance of the effects of hemp cultivars (CV), type (CDs) and their interactions on parameters determined in Trial 2#, data was statistically analyzed using a general linear model (GLM) with SPSS statistics v.26.0 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, USA). If a significant difference among treatments was detected at p=0.05, multiple comparison was performed using Tukey’s test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of carbon dots synthesized from citric acid and hemp stem retting water
ce:section.ce:para: Both CA-CDs and HP-CDs emitted fluorescence, with the fluorescence intensity of HP-CDs being slightly weaker than that of CA-CDs ( Fig. 2). The excitation wavelength of CA-CDs ranged from 310 nm to 510 nm, while that of HP-CDs ranged from 290 nm to 450 nm. Furthermore, both CDs showed strong emission peaks around 455 nm at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and 370 nm. Raman spectra of HP-CDs and CA-CDs showed distinct G-peaks at 1612 cm−1, and 1597 cm−1, respectively ( Fig. 3). The distinct G-peaks suggested a high graphitization degree of the synthesized CDs.

The FT-IR spectra ( Fig. 4) showed CA-CDs uptake band at 3022 cm −1 (O-H bond), 1691 cm−1 (CO bond), 1403 cm−1 (C-H bond), and 1171 cm−1 (C-O bond), while HP-CDs showed negative peaks at 3416 cm−1, 1615 cm−1, 1385 cm−1, 1142 cm−1, which corresponded to bonds of O-H/N-H, CC/CO, C-O/C-N/O-H, C-O, respectively. Elemental information of the synthesized CDs was characterized using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) ( Fig. 5). There were two predominant characteristic peaks in the XPS spectrum of CA-CDs, corresponding to O 1 s and C 1 s. The high-resolution spectrum indicated that the O 1 s consist of CO (531.66 eV) and C-O (532.91 eV), while the C 1 s consist of C-C (284.87 eV), C-O (286.57 eV) and CO (288.72 eV). Similar peaks of O 1 s and C 1 s were detected in the XPS spectrum of HP-CDs. The high-resolution spectrum indicated that the O 1 s consist of O-N (531.19 eV) and O-H (532.26 eV), while O 1 s consist of C-C/CC (284.73 eV), C-OH/C-O-C (286.22 eV) and C-O (288.03 eV). In addition to the O 1 s and C 1 s peaks, there was a N 1 s peak in the HP-CDs XPS spectrum (Fig. 5A), which corresponds to the presence of graphitic N (400.01 eV) and pyridinic N (399.42 eV) (Fig. 5D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of carbon dots synthesized from citric acid and hemp stem retting water on the growth of hemp seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Six concentration levels of CA-CDs and HP-CDs were separately applied to hemp seedlings in Trial 1#. Overall, the interactions between CD type and cultivar for all the measured parameters were not significant (p>0.05; Table 2). CDs treatments did not significantly affect plant height (p>0.05), stem diameter (p>0.05), and stem dry weight (p>0.05).

In Trial 1#, the application of CDs significantly affected leaf (p＜0.001) and root (p＜0.01) dry weight (Table 2), with significant interaction between concentration and CD type for both parameters. For both CA-CDs and HP-CDs treatment, leaf dry weight was increased under C10 and C100concentrations, with average value of two tested cultivars at C100 being 118.52 % of C0 for CA-CDs and 122.22 % for HP-CDs ( Fig. 6). However, the leaf dry weight of both tested cultivars showed a decreasing trend when the concentration of CA-CDs exceeded C500, with an average value dropping to 70.37 % of C0 at C2000 (Fig. 6A, B). Additionally, leaf edges showed symptoms of necrosis when the CA-CDs concentration reached C500 ( Fig. 7C, D). In contrast, increasing the concentration of HP-CDs from C10 to C2000 had little negative effects on leaf morphology and dry weight (Fig. 6A, B, Fig. 7A, B). The response of the root to CA-CDs and HP-CDs application was similar to that of the leaf ( Figs. 7 and 8). With the application of CA-CDs, root dry weight increased by 21.15 % at C10 and 24.99 % at C100, averaged across two cultivars. However, it decreased by 15.39 %, 11.54 %, and 8.34 % at C500, C1000, and C2000, respectively, compared to C0 (Fig. 6C, D). The root volume and dry weight of the plants treated with HP-CDs had no significant difference as the concentration increased from C10 to C2000 (Fig. 6C, D, and Fig. 8).

As shown in Trial 1#, CA-CDs had an optimal effect on hemp growth at a concentration of C100. Therefore, this concentration was selected for further investigation into the effect of CA-CDs and HP-CDs on biomass weight and nitrogen uptake of hemp seedlings in Trial 2#. In Trial 2#, CA-CDs and HP-CDs significantly affected the weight of root (p<0.01), leaf (p<0.001), and stem (p<0.01), with no significant interaction (p>0.05) between CDs type and cultivar. On average across the two cultivars, CA-CDs increased the dry weight of root, leaf, and stem by 56.3 %, 43.4 %, and 54.1 %, respectively, compared to CK ( Fig. 9). The effect of HP-CDs was slightly less pronounced (p>0.05), resulting in increases of 47.2 %, 36.4 % and 37.8 % for root, leaf and stem dry weight, respectively, compared to CK.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of carbon dots on the physiological and biochemical characteristics of hemp seedling
ce:section.ce:para: To understand how CA-CDs and HP-CDs affect the physiological and biochemical characteristics of hemp seedlings, SPAD value, soluble sugar concentration (SS), soluble protein concentration (SP), dismutase activity (SOD), and malondialdehyde concentration (MDA) of leaves were measured at the 3rd day after the last CDs application in Trial 1# ( Table 3). CDs treatment significantly affected the SPAD value of leaves (p<0.05), with plants treated with CA-CDs having a 3.14 % higher SPAD value compared to those treated with HP-CDs. On average, CDs concentration at C10 and C100 increased the leaf SPAD value by 8.0 %, and10.8 %, respectively, when compared to C0.

In general, CDs treatment did not induce significant changes in SS and SP of leaves in Trial 1#, but significantly affected the SOD activity and MDA concentration in leaves (Table 3). Significant interactions between CDs and concentration were observed for SOD activity and MDA concentration in leaves (Table 3). Compared to C0, the activity of SOD in leaves showed little change when CA-CDs were applied at C10 and C100 concentrations, but increased by 274.64 %, 279.58 %, and 258.50 % at C500, C1000, and C2000 concentrations, respectively ( Fig. 10). In line with the response of SOD activity, MDA concentration varied little with the application of CA-CDs at C10 and C100, while it increased 9.04∼22.39 % as the concentration of CA-CDs increased from C500 to C2000. In contrast, HP-CDs treatments did not induce a significant change in leaf SOD activity and MDA concentration (Fig. 10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of carbon dots on nitrogen uptake in hemp seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of CDs on nitrogen uptake in hemp plants were assessed in Trial 2#, using a concentration of 100 mg·L−1 (same as C100 in the first experiment) for both CA-CDs and HP-CDs treatments and 0 mg·L−1 as the control (CK).

CDs treatment significantly influenced nitrogen (N) content in root (p<0.001), leaf (p<0.001), and stem (p<0.01), with no significant interaction observed between cultivar and treatment ( Table 4). On average, HP-CDs increased the N content in root, leaf, and stem of both cultivars by 7.7 %, 34.4 %, and 25.6 %, respectively, compared to CK. Similarly, compared with CK, CA-CDs decreased the N content in the root by 20.4 %, while increasing the N content in the leaf and stem by 14.1 % and 27.1 %, respectively. Plants treated with CDs showed higher nitrogen uptake in all organs, resulting in a higher total nitrogen uptake compared to those grown under CK conditions (Table 4). Notably, in both cultivars, plants treated with HP-CDs exhibited the highest nitrogen uptake in the roots and stems compared to CK, with increases of 59.1 % and 82.7 %, respectively. In contrast, CA-CDs had the highest nitrogen uptake in the leaf, with an average increase of 93.1 % compared to CK.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of carbon dots derived from hemp stem retting water
ce:section.ce:para: CDs derived from hemp retting water (HP-CDs) displayed excitation-dependent fluorescence, in line with most reported CDs (Cheng et al., 2023; Tejwan et al., 2020). This phenomenon can largely be attributed to uneven surface chemistry and various size distributions. Similar to CDs synthesized from citric acid (CA-CDs), HP-CDs showed a wide range of emission spectra (Fig. 2A), demonstrating their strong fluorescence characteristics. Chloroplasts effectively absorb light at wavelengths ranging from 400 to 500 nm and from 650 to 700 nm (Evans et al., 2004; Hu et al., 2022). Both CA-CDs and HP-CDs featured strong emission peaks around 455 nm at excitation wavelengths of 350 nm and 370 nm (Fig. 2), closely aligning with the uptake spectrum of chloroplasts in the blue light region. This suggests their potential to convert UV light into photosynthetically active light that can be efficiently utilized by chloroplasts (Chandra et al., 2014), thereby enhancing photosynthetic efficiency (Hu et al., 2022), and ultimately contributing to biomass accumulation.

HP-CDs exhibited a weaker fluorescence intensity compared to that of CA-CDs (Fig. 2). This relatively weaker fluorescence intensity has also been reported for other natural resource-based CDs, including those synthesized from grape pomace (Murru et al., 2020) and pollens (Zheng et al., 2017). The weaker fluorescence intensity of HP-CDs could be attributed to their complex structure, as shown by the spectrum of FITR and XPS (Figs. 4, 5). Nevertheless, the fluorescence intensity of HP-CDs could be improved through surface modifications, such as plasma or microwave-assisted technology, if required for specific applications (Liu et al., 2024).

Moreover, nitrogen incorporation is a characteristic feature often observed in biomass-derived CDs (Cheng et al., 2023). In this study, the FTIR and XPS analysis revealed the presence of N in HP-CDs, primarily presented in the form of graphitic and pyridinic structures (Fig. 5). Both graphitic and pyridinic nitrogen can enhance electron availability, especially the pyridinic nitrogen. Such nitrogen configuration was suggested to facilitate electron transfer in photosystem II (Hu et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2021a), thereby supporting the potential application of HP-CDs as effective agents for enhancing photosynthetic efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots derived from hemp stem retting water were less phytotoxic than citric acid-derived
ce:section.ce:para: The enhancement effect of CDs on plant growth have been reported in many crop plants, including mung bean(Wang et al., 2018), gram plant (Wang et al., 2022), wheat (Tripathi and Sarkar, 2015), and rice (Li et al., 2020). However, inhibition of the growth of crops was also observed in several studies, especially when CDs were applied in higher concentrations. The root and shoot weights of maize plants significantly reduced after incubated with citric acid-derived CDs (CA-CDs) at 1000 and 2000 mg L−1(Chen et al., 2016), while the root elongation of Arabidopsis thaliana was inhibited when the CA-CDs concentration exceeded 125 mg L−1 (Chen et al., 2018).

In this study, we examined the dose-response effects of CA-CDs and HP-CDs on the phytotoxicity related to the growth of hemp seedlings in Trial 1#. The effects of six concentrations (0, 10, 100, 500, 1000, 2000 mg⋅L−1) of CA-CDs and HP-CDs on seedling growth were assessed over a short application period of 12 days. Results indicated that CA-CDs exhibited concentration-dependent phytotoxicity, with low concentrations (10 and 100 mg L−1) promoting the growth of hemp seedlings while higher concentrations (e.g. 500–2000 mg L−1) resulted in reduced dry weight accumulation (Table 2; Fig. 6). This decline can be attributed to the detrimental effect of CA-CDs on hemp, as evidenced by necrotic symptoms observed at the leaf margins (Fig. 7C and D) and a clear reduction in root volume (Fig. 8C and D). The necrotic symptoms observed in hemp leaves may be attributed to the oxidative stress stimulated by high concentrations of CA-CDs, as evidenced by a significant increase in the activity of superoxide dismutase (SOD) and malondialdehyde (MDA) concentration in leaves (Table 3; Fig. 10).

In contrast, HP-CDs did not show any phytotoxicity, even at an applied concentration of 2000 mg L−1 (Fig. 10). This indicates that CDs derived from hemp retting wastwater may be considered safe for agricultural applications. According to the guidelines from the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (1996), nanoparticles are generally regarded as having minimal toxicity if they do not affect seed germination and root growth at a concentration of 2000 mg L−1. Further research is needed to fully evaluate the safety and potential benefits of using HP-CDs in agriculture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots derived from hemp stem retting water promoted plant growth
ce:section.ce:para: The results from Trial 1# indicated that both CA-CDs demonstrated optimal performance at a concentration of 100 mg·L−1. Therefore, in Trial 2#, the effectof CDs on plant growth and nitrogen uptake was assessed using a concentration of 100 mg·L−1 (C100), with 0 mg·L−1 as the control (CK).

In consistent with the results of Trial 1#, both CA-CDs and HP-CDs promoted the dry weight accumulation of hemp plants in Trial 2# (Fig. 9). On one hand, as mentioned in the previous section, both CDs displayed wide range of emission spectra, which could convert UV light into photosynthetically active light and thereby enhancing their effectiveness in promoting photosynthetic processes in plants. On the other hand, both CA-CDs and HP-CDs possess hydrophilic groups on their surfaces (Fig. 4), which serve as binding sites for water molecules, facilitating the uptake of water and nutrients by the roots (Wang et al., 2018), and thereby promoting hemp seedling growth in this study. This is further supported by our observation that the application of CDs enhanced the nitrogen content and uptake in the root, leaf and stem of hemp plants (Table 4). However, it is worthnoting that both CDs exhibited similar effects on the growth parameter, even though the fluorescence intensity of CA-CDs was higher than HP-CDs (Fig. 2). The potentially weaker effect of the lower fluorescence intensity of HP-CDs on plant growth could be offset by the additional N element in the HP-CDs (Fig. 5). Nevertheless, further study is needed to quantify the contribution of wavelength conversion and nitrogen uptake to biomass accumulation. It is also interesting to explore the phytoactivity of N in HP-CDs as they may be trapped by the complex structure of CDs.

Carbon dots (CDs) derived from hemp retting water (HP-CDs) were found to be non-phytotoxic even at a high concentration of 2000 mg L−1, when citric acid derived carbon dots (CA-CDs) showed adverse effects on plant growth, including increased activity of superoxide dismutase (SOD) and increased concentration of malondialdehyde (MDA) in leaves, necrotic symptom at the leaf edges, reduced root volume, and decreased dry weight accumulation. Moreover, in line with CA-CDs, HP-CDs exhibited the potential to convert short wavelength light into photosynthetically active light, facilitate the uptake of nitrogen, and thereby promoting plant growth and biomass accumulation. Overall, this research highlights the potential of HP-CDs as an effective agent for promoting plant growth while having low phytotoxic effects. Further research is needed to fully understand the underlying mechanisms and optimize the application of HP-CDs in agriculture.

Chunyu Ji: Resources, Methodology. Guanghui Du: Writing – review & editing. Saddam Hussain: Writing – review & editing. Zhili Peng: Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Kailei Tang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jiafeng Wu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Wenjing Ouyang: Writing – review & editing. Gonglin Chen: Resources, Methodology. Song Yuan: Resources, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by Natural Science Foundation of Yunnan province, China under grant number 202201AT070188; China Agriculture Research System of MOF and MARA under grant number CARS-16-E15.
